so my name is anne campbell i am the
primary author of cognitive complexity i
have coded
in a number of languages and i brought
all of that experience along with the
experience of my colleagues
into writing cognitive complexity
now if you're familiar with complexity
in a programming context you're probably
most familiar
and have most used cyclomatic complexity
that was
released in or published in december of
and its aim was to measure
understandability what it really does
well is
measure the number of test cases that
you need
to fully unit test a section of code
what it really doesn't get at is
understandability
and at center source we really felt a
need for a metric that would help us
gauge that
so that's why we undertook cognitive
complexity
now i want to illustrate that with a
couple of examples here
so first we start with sum of primes and
these are examples are straight from the
white paper
so sum of primes um has a cyclomatic
complexity of four
so plus one for the method structure
plus one for each four
and plus one for the if total of four
now contrast that with
get words so again plus one for the
method structure and then
plus one for each case in the switch for
cyclomatic complexity of
four so these two methods have equal
cyclomatic complexity
um but i think it's obvious that they
don't have equal
understandability and that's why we felt
the need to
create something else now cyclomatic
complexity
uh it works off of mathematical formulas
and so i think you see here that for
judging
understandability mathematical formulas
don't really do it so instead
cognitive complexity has guiding
principles
so the first guiding principle is to
ignore structures that allow multiple
statements to be readably shorthanded
into one statement the primary example
of that is the method structure itself
assuming you put a good name on your
method
then that's what you've done when you
create a method so we're not going to
increment for the method
we also don't increment for things like
the null coalescing operator that some
languages have
that that's once you understand the
syntax that's really easy to read
and so we don't increment for it we do
increment
for each break in the linear flow of the
code the idea here is that
ideally you would be able to read code
like a novel
top to bottom left to right in an
unbroken flow
when that flow is broken when you have
to do something other than
top to bottom left to right then we
increment for that because it makes you
work harder
we also increment when flow breaking
structures are nested
so i think it's intuitively obvious here
that if i have five
if statements in a row that's easier to
understand
than if i have those five if statements
successively nested
and that heart that that extra work that
you have to do to understand that
nesting
is what we're trying to take into
account when we increment
for those for the nesting now a lot of
people want to know okay well
give me the specification so really
quickly here i'm going to go through it
we increment four breaks in the linear
flow for
branches so if else if else ternary
catch and switch
for loops so four for each while do
while and each method in a recursion
cycle
yes recursion is a loop it's sort of a
metal loop but it's a loop
we take that into account we increment
four jumps to labels so go to break and
continue to a label
and we increment for sequences of like
binary logical
operators for nesting
these are the things that increment the
nesting level
so if else if else ternary catch switch
four four each wild do while and nested
methods and method like structures such
as lambdas now again we don't increment
for having a method
but when you have a method inside of a
method or a lambda inside of a method
then we start counting the nesting
these are the things that receive a
nesting increment
if ternary switch catch four for each
while
do while this will all make a little bit
more sense when we start looking at
code there are exceptions to these rules
so for instance cobalt does not have an
elsif structure typically what you'll
see
in cobalt is an if with an else
and there's nothing inside the else but
another if with an else
and there's nothing inside the else but
another if with an else
so not having that structure doesn't
mean that cobalt programmers don't have
the need
they have to work around the limitations
of their language
and if we incremented in cobol for that
type of structure
it would really just be unfair so we're
not going to penalize cobalt developers
because of what their language doesn't
have so there's an exception for that
similarly up until relatively recently
javascript
didn't have a class structure and so
javascript programmers
used sort of a pseudo-class structure
even today there's a lot of excellent

class structure and there are a number
of frameworks that still today require

program with a pseudo class structure
and so there's
a specific exception for javascript
similarly there's an exception for

and we consider additional exceptions
as we come across needs in specific
languages but all of the exceptions are
language specific what's not included in
cognitive complexity
is hard language features such as

templates and generics multiple
inheritance etc
the reason for this is that that way

once you start getting into four and

they also said that since developers

a large portion of their time in

these two things help you reduce a lot

this is when you have an if with nothing

fullText: all right hello and welcome to refactoring with cognitive complexity thanks for joining us i'll get started now i'm going to start with some housekeeping details first this will be a 45-minute session including demo and q a please don't go ahead and cue your questions in the q a box we've got colin and aaron from the sonar source services group to cue your questions triage them and feed them to me at the end we are recording this presentation and in a few days you'll get an email with a link to the recording and all the q a so even if i don't get to your question live you will get a link to the answer afterwards and finally once the webinar ends you'll get a feedback survey please do take the survey please tell me how i did tell me what you liked what i need to work on and what sonar source should cover in future webinars okay so the agenda is i'll start with some introductions i'll talk about why we formulated cognitive complexity then we'll look at what cognitive complexity is we'll talk about the validation that's out there then we'll get into refactoring why we're all here and then we'll talk about what's next so my name is anne campbell i am the primary author of cognitive complexity i have coded in a number of languages and i brought all of that experience along with the experience of my colleagues into writing cognitive complexity now if you're familiar with complexity in a programming context you're probably most familiar and have most used cyclomatic complexity that was released in or published in december of 1976 and its aim was to measure understandability what it really does well is measure the number of test cases that you need to fully unit test a section of code what it really doesn't get at is understandability and at center source we really felt a need for a metric that would help us gauge that so that's why we undertook cognitive complexity now i want to illustrate that with a couple of examples here so first we start with sum of primes and these are examples are straight from the white paper so sum of primes um has a cyclomatic complexity of four so plus one for the method structure plus one for each four and plus one for the if total of four now contrast that with get words so again plus one for the method structure and then plus one for each case in the switch for cyclomatic complexity of four so these two methods have equal cyclomatic complexity um but i think it's obvious that they don't have equal understandability and that's why we felt the need to create something else now cyclomatic complexity uh it works off of mathematical formulas and so i think you see here that for judging understandability mathematical formulas don't really do it so instead cognitive complexity has guiding principles so the first guiding principle is to ignore structures that allow multiple statements to be readably shorthanded into one statement the primary example of that is the method structure itself assuming you put a good name on your method then that's what you've done when you create a method so we're not going to increment for the method we also don't increment for things like the null coalescing operator that some languages have that that's once you understand the syntax that's really easy to read and so we don't increment for it we do increment for each break in the linear flow of the code the idea here is that ideally you would be able to read code like a novel top to bottom left to right in an unbroken flow when that flow is broken when you have to do something other than top to bottom left to right then we increment for that because it makes you work harder we also increment when flow breaking structures are nested so i think it's intuitively obvious here that if i have five if statements in a row that's easier to understand than if i have those five if statements successively nested and that heart that that extra work that you have to do to understand that nesting is what we're trying to take into account when we increment for those for the nesting now a lot of people want to know okay well give me the specification so really quickly here i'm going to go through it we increment four breaks in the linear flow for branches so if else if else ternary catch and switch for loops so four for each while do while and each method in a recursion cycle yes recursion is a loop it's sort of a metal loop but it's a loop we take that into account we increment four jumps to labels so go to break and continue to a label and we increment for sequences of like binary logical operators for nesting these are the things that increment the nesting level so if else if else ternary catch switch four four each wild do while and nested methods and method like structures such as lambdas now again we don't increment for having a method but when you have a method inside of a method or a lambda inside of a method then we start counting the nesting these are the things that receive a nesting increment if ternary switch catch four for each while do while this will all make a little bit more sense when we start looking at code there are exceptions to these rules so for instance cobalt does not have an elsif structure typically what you'll see in cobalt is an if with an else and there's nothing inside the else but another if with an else and there's nothing inside the else but another if with an else so not having that structure doesn't mean that cobalt programmers don't have the need they have to work around the limitations of their language and if we incremented in cobol for that type of structure it would really just be unfair so we're not going to penalize cobalt developers because of what their language doesn't have so there's an exception for that similarly up until relatively recently javascript didn't have a class structure and so javascript programmers used sort of a pseudo-class structure even today there's a lot of excellent code that uses that pseudo cluster class structure and there are a number of frameworks that still today require you to program with a pseudo class structure and so there's a specific exception for javascript similarly there's an exception for decorators in python and we consider additional exceptions as we come across needs in specific languages but all of the exceptions are language specific what's not included in cognitive complexity is hard language features such as pointer and direction templates and generics multiple inheritance etc the reason for this is that that way lies religious wars so i used to program c for me two levels of pointer and direction is routine once you start getting into four and more then it starts getting hard but deciding where it gets hard is really very much a judgment call and we just didn't want to get into that so hard language features are not included in cognitive complexity because cognitive complexity is about control flow so this is really about how hard is the control flow of your code to understand so coming back to my examples and and i said that this would start to make sense once we could started applying this to code so coming back to sum of primes let's look at the cognitive complexity so we've got this first for loop which is plus one for the four then we've inside that we've got another for loop nested so that's plus two one for the four one for the nesting then inside of that we've got an if that's plus three one for the if two for the nesting we don't do nesting on the jump tool label so our continue to the out label is just plus one and that gives us a total cognitive complexity on this method of seven now contrast that with get words where we've got a switch and three cases and a default and that's plus one for the switch in all its cases and so we have a total cognitive complexity on this method of one and so now just looking at the numbers i can understand immediately which of these is going to be more difficult to understand and that's what we were trying to get at with cognitive complexity so validation um up until last summer you could have very um validly said to me it's just your opinion you know how do you know that this that this works um so i was very relieved last summer to see that a paper was published which actually looked at the validity of cognitive complexity and the authors found that it is actually a good measure of source code understandability i've listed some quotes from the paper here so the authors found that cognitive complexity is a good indicator of how long it takes developers to understand a piece of code and that code snippets with higher cognitive complexity are rated by developers as more difficult to understand they also said that since developers spend a large time a large portion of their time in maintenance very few of us get to write greenfield code that it's worthwhile to make the code easier to understand and that cognitive complexity can help you find the pieces of code that need work they also said that it's not proven yet how much cognitive complexity is too much cognitive complexity personally i'm very much looking forward to someone taking this topic up and publishing a follow-up paper fingers crossed so now what we're all here for refactoring so the reason that this is mainly focused on refactoring is that i personally don't find it particularly helpful to be able to point at something and say that's bad without knowing how to make it better so that's why that's the primary focus here in the years since we published cognitive complexity i've come up with a few different patterns that are seem to be the best ways to approach reducing cognitive complexity and therefore making the code easier to read so the first is invert a condition and use an early exit sort of corollary to that is eliminating the else clause these two things help you reduce a lot of nesting the next thing is structural collapse now this is not what happens in an earthquake this is when you have an if with nothing inside it but another if and potentially even more than that and so what you can do is and those conditions together and only have a single if makes it easier to understand um the next one is extracting methods so sometimes you're looking at a method with a gnarly piece of code in the middle that you can't do much with except extract it to a new method and put a readable name on it so again do readably shorthand multiple lines into one that can be very helpful sometimes similarly if you're doing the same thing over and over again then that's a cause for extracting a helper method and finally language features so there's nothing specific here this is about knowing what the features are in your language that are easier to understand than other ways of doing it you know as programmers we know that there's very often multiple different ways to write the code and so this final one isn't about knowing which ways are easier for the person who comes behind you to understand so it's time to get into refactoring my job here is to make things wait this is the wrong gift my job is not to make things as difficult as possible my job is in fact to make things as easy as possible so let's do this so i'm going to first jump into whoops did not mean to do that sonar cloud so central cloud is an online service to provide static analysis to help you improve the code quality and code security of your projects and i'm looking at an issue in an open source project sonar cloud is free for open source projects and i've got an issue here a cognitive complexity issue and i've got secondary locations to help me understand where the cognitive complexity is coming from so for instance there's plus one on this else else's don't receive an sc increment we've already paid that penalty on the if so this if is plus four one for the f3 for the nesting all the way down here to secondary location number 20 which is plus 10. and so i've got a total cognitive complexity on this method that's 65 and when i look at what the method is i see i start with an if and i have an else with nothing in the else but another if and an else with nothing in the else but another if and so on and so um that pattern may sound familiar to you from when i was talking about the exception in cobol because in cobalt you don't have the elsif structure but this is java and you do have the lcf structure and so there's no exception and so we count up all of this cognitive complexity and it adds up really fast so this is a great example and they're not usually this clear-cut this is a great example of a place where structural collapse can really help what we what you can do here is turn this all into a single if else if tree and i counted it up once i think it adds up to 13 if you were to collapse this all into a single tree and that includes the plus one for our binary logical operator sequence so that's what i wanted to show you in the browser now i'm going to jump into the ide here so first i am in intellij's idea and i'm using sonar lens centerlint is a free and open source ide plugin it's available for most of the intellij ides as well as eclipse visual studio and vs code so down here at the bottom i've got the sonar lint pane open and it's showing me all of the current all of the issues that are in my current file so i'm going to click on my cognitive complexity issue and it takes me to the issue in the code and it highlights my secondary locations for me it also shows me my secondary locations here on the right now by default the first tab in this part of the pane is the rule itself in case i don't understand quite what's going on but i can also have easy access to the secondary locations and i can see the message for each location i can also see the message if i mouse over it so here at this first four it's plus one by the time i get down here to this if it's plus five now this issue i'm at 16 versus the 15 threshold allowed that's the default threshold that we set in the rule for java and anytime you have a rule that includes a numeric threshold the threshold is going to be somewhat arbitrary and so it would be really easy to look at this and say well 16 isn't that much worse than 15. do we really need to do something here and you know sometimes that's going to be the best answer but it's worth looking at it first to see if there's something simple you can do to clean it up before you move on and so when i look at this method and i look at where the cognitive complexity is coming from i see this deeply nested indenting here um and and here in the secondary locations list i see that most of the cognitive complexity is coming from nesting and so if i can reduce some of that nesting i can very easily get below the threshold and so i start here at the top with a for loop there's not a lot i can do with this for loop so you know a lot of times when you're dealing with loops your best option is to extract the code inside to another method but this is already you know a nice compact method this is not a huge method it's just that there's a lot of
